content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 2, 2017


Mayor Elizabeth Gibbons
City of Campbell
70 North First Street
Campbell, CA 95008

Re:     Your Request for Advice
         Our File No. A-17-240

Dear Ms. Gibbons:

        This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

        Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

        As Mayor and Councilmember of the City of Campbell, do you have a disqualifying conflict of interest in participating in governmental decisions between the City and the Campbell Unified School District regarding a proposed project when you own your personal residence, located within 500 feet of the middle school property line?

CONCLUSION

        Yes, you have a disqualifying conflict of interest in participating in project-related decisions. You have not presented sufficient facts indicating that there will not be a reasonably foreseeable measurable impact as a result from the decisions.




_________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-240
Page No. 2

FACTS

The Campbell Unified School District operates a middle school in the City of Campbell. In
1972, the City entered a Joint Use Agreement with the School District. The agreement governs the
joint use of a portion of land encompassing the middle school site for recreation purposes.
Specifically, the agreement requires that a portion of the middle school site be considered a "joint
park and recreation-school site to be constructed, operated, used and maintained in accordance with
this agreement."

You stated that the School District is currently planning changes to the middle school
campus, which may encroach into the Joint Use Agreement site. The School District projects are
approved by the Division of the State Architect. The City has notified the School District of the
Agreement to avoid potential infringement into the Joint Use Agreement site.

For the 2016-2017 academic year, the middle school provided educational services to
students from fifth to eighth grade. You stated that the past population of the school ranged between
650-750 students. For the 2017-2018 academic year, the school will provide seventh and eighth
grade education to approximately 622 students.

You indicated that the School District intends to convert the current seventh and eighth
grade middle school into a pre-Kindergarten to eighth grade school within the next few years. You
estimate that a school educating 10 grades with 30 students per class and three classrooms each
could potentially expand the campus population to 900 students. However, the total population on
the proposed pre-K to eighth grade campus is unclear. You currently live on the only residential
street that can access the campus, seven houses away from the school.

You have raised concerns that the project may eliminate a baseball and several softball and
soccer fields located in J.D. Morgan Park, a portion of land governed by the Joint Use Agreement.
According to you, the centrally-located park is extensively used by the community. The fields
currently generate park maintenance funds for the City, and the park is incorporated into the City's
General Plan and open space calculation.

You speculate that property values in the City will likely be impacted by the reduction of
usable recreational space as a result of the proposed development. You further speculate that traffic
in your neighborhood and surrounding streets will change. Although, the School District has not
provided empirical data regarding any potential impacts on traffic to confirm your speculation.

ANALYSIS

Under Section 87100, a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes,
participates in making, or uses his or her position to influence a governmental decision that has a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests.

Section 87103(b) indicates that an official has an interest in "[a]ny real property in which
the public official has a direct or indirect interest of at least $2,000." Your real property interest in
your residence is the only interest implicated by the decisions. Accordingly, we must determine if
the decisions will have a foreseeable and material effect.

File No. A-17-240
Page No. 3

Foreseeability:

The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. (Regulation 1870l.) An interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the governmental decision. (Regulation 1870l(a).) Under the facts you have provided, your residence is not explicitly involved in the decisions. Thus, Regulation 1870l(b) provides: "[i]n general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable."²

Materiality:

The financial effects of project-related decisions on your real property interest are material if the decisions:

• Would consider any decision affecting real property value located within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property, other than commercial property . . .. Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission may provide written advice allowing an official to participate under these circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property. (Regulation 18702.2(a)(11).)

You own your residence on the only access street to the school. You posited that traffic in your neighborhood and surrounding streets will change as a result of the proposed project. You also speculate that City property values will likely be impacted by the reduction of usable recreational space in the park. Both an increase in traffic levels and a reduction of usable recreational space are reasonably foreseeable material effects of the project, and you have not presented facts indicating to the contrary. As your residence is within 500 feet of the School District's property line, you have a disqualifying conflict of interest in participating in project-related decisions. (Regulation 18702.2(a)(11).)³

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

[signature] By: Ryan P. O'Connor
Counsel, Legal Division

___________________________

² Regulation 1870l(b) also provides a non-exhaustive list of six factors that may be considered in the "reasonably foreseeable" determination.

³ Note that a city councilmember, an office specified in Section 87200, with a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting must: (1) publicly identify each financial interest that gives rise to the disqualifying conflict of interest, (2) recuse him- or herself from the decision, and; (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. (Section 87105; Regulation 18707.)

sections.question:
As Mayor and Councilmember of the City of Campbell, do you have a disqualifying conflict 
of interest in participating in governmental decisions between the City and the Campbell Unified 
School District regarding a proposed project when you own your personal residence, located within 
500 feet of the middle school property line?
sections.conclusion:
Yes, you have a disqualifying conflict of interest in participating in project-related 
decisions. You have not presented sufficient facts indicating that there will not be a reasonably 
foreseeable measurable impact as n result from the decisions.
